items.title: Ozone depletion began decades before discovery of ozone hole, scientists find
items.summary: The Antarctic ozone hole was discovered in 1985, when scientists observed a severe depletion in Earth's protective layer of stratospheric ozone. Industrial chemicals known as chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), then widely used as refrigerants, propellants, foam-blowing agents and solvents, were at the

items.title: Can climate shocks change how people feel about paying taxes?
items.summary: Climate-related disasters are becoming more frequent and more intense across sub-Saharan Africa. Floods, droughts, heat waves and storms are no longer isolated environmental events. They increasingly shape livelihoods, inequality, public trust, and the relationship between citizens and the state.

items.title: There may be 3 times more insect species than previously thought
items.summary: A new estimate of insect species globally finds that there may be 8 million to 14 million more species than people thought, with few of them discovered.

items.title: Sweltering Midwest heat cancels outdoor plans as cooling centers open and the East braces
items.summary: Summer camp and other outdoor activities were canceled or delayed Monday as a heat wave held its grip on the Midwest and spread eastward. Communities opened cooling centers and urged people to take it easy and stay hydrated.

items.title: As communities face more frequent hazard warnings, we need better systems to avoid 'emergency fatigue'
items.summary: Earlier this month, Wellington declared a local state of emergency, including evacuation orders, when forecast powerful swells threatened to inundate coastal properties.

items.title: Bronze Age boat carvings point to maritime links from Iberia to Scandinavia
items.summary: Bronze Age rock carvings suggest communities across Europe were far more connected than previously thought, according to a new study led by the Department of Archaeology. The research compared ancient rock carvings, or "petroglyphs," of boats across 12 sites in Northwest Iberia (modern Spain and Por

items.title: 40°C in Paris: extreme summer heat is no longer exceptional for most of Europe
items.summary: Heat waves are Europe's biggest climate threat. These episodes of extreme heat compound rising average air temperatures across the world, all of which is the direct result of greenhouse gas emissions.

items.title: A goat's tooth may have solved a 100‑year debate about ancient Greek farming
items.summary: The agricultural economy was the backbone of wealth in ancient Greece. Food brought people together, whether in smaller groups at a wine-drinking symposium or the entire community in a sacrificial feast of epic proportions. In The Odyssey, the ancient Greek epic poem, Odysseus' son joins one of

items.title: Spiders benefit from seemingly monotonous forests
items.summary: In ecology, the principle holds that the more diverse and heterogeneous a habitat is, the more different species it supports. To promote species diversity in forests, clearings are therefore created for nature conservation purposes, or deadwood is deliberately left in place. For many species, such a

items.title: Heat is destroying Australia's underwater forests. Seaweed biobanks could help save them
items.summary: Australia's Great Southern Reef is built not by coral but by seaweed. The seaweed forests on these rocky reefs stretch more than 8,000 kilometers (5,000 miles) around southern Australia.

items.title: Nova V612 Scuti's light curve becomes audio, revealing how stellar shocks evolved
items.summary: Researchers in the Department of Physics & Astronomy at Texas Tech University recently used audio to represent the spectacular explosion of a star in deep space while also delving into the data to better understand how the phenomenon unfolded.

items.title: What universities are getting wrong about teaching in the age of AI
items.summary: It's an understatement that educators worry about students using AI to offload the cognitive struggle that is critical for learning. That worry is well founded.

items.title: Psychologists survey students to determine what they really think about social media
items.summary: The first findings from a major survey of more than 800 young people ages 11–17 about social media were revealed to local schoolchildren today by psychology researchers at an event on the University of Kent's Canterbury campus. Dr. Lindsey Cameron and Dr. Katie Goodbun launched The Alternative

items.title: Switching spin states in manganese ions with light opens new path for molecular memory
items.summary: Researchers at Johannes Gutenberg University Mainz (JGU) have developed a new way to use molecules as tiny data storage devices with a new manganese-based material. Until now, this was possible only with iron-containing molecular materials, which require very low temperatures—ranging from 100 to a m

items.title: Unintended climate trade-off: Clean air policies intensify urban heat island in humid cities, study finds
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Hong Kong (HKU) have uncovered a critical, underrecognized trade-off in global environmental policies: While essential for improving public health, large-scale air pollution reductions are unintentionally intensifying warm-season surface urban heat islands (UHI) in h

items.title: Uncovering the trigger behind slow earthquakes
items.summary: New research led by the University of New England's Dr. Timothy Chapman has uncovered the trigger behind slow earthquakes, providing valuable answers for those living in disaster-prone areas. The research has been published in Geology.

items.title: Solar storms leave their mark on cosmic rays that reach Earth
items.summary: A new study has revealed an unexpected link between solar storms and the flux of high-energy cosmic rays arriving at Earth. The findings, made using one of the world's largest cosmic ray detectors, could open up a new way to probe the magnetic structures inside solar storms—and potentially impr

items.title: Hawaiian short-eared owl deaths in Hawaiʻi primarily caused by vehicle collisions
items.summary: Trauma from vehicle collisions caused the majority of documented deaths for the Pueo (Hawaiian short-eared owl), according to a statewide study led by researchers at the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa. The findings represent the most extensive mortality assessment to date for this culturally signifi

items.title: First ever dinosaur found in Antarctica described for science
items.summary: The first dinosaur fossil found on the Antarctic continent has been described scientifically. The fossil, a vertebra, was found on a British Antarctic Survey (BAS) expedition in 1985 but has only recently been recognized as that of a dinosaur. The paper, "A titanosaurian sauropod dinosaur from the L

items.title: COVID-era renter protection law slashed Virginia evictions, research finds
items.summary: In 2020, Virginia received approximately $1 billion through a federal COVID-era emergency rental assistance program, which aimed to motivate landlords nationwide to postpone evictions of tenants who could no longer pay rent due to the pandemic. But in Virginia, lawmakers did not simply rely on landl

items.title: Why people worldwide see some mental abilities as inborn and others as learned
items.summary: When does a child begin to reason? When do they develop self-control? Are some mental abilities present from birth, while others are acquired through experience? Questions like these have fascinated philosophers, educators and scientists for centuries. Yet surprisingly little is known about how ordi

items.title: For hiring, remote work means more expertise, research finds
items.summary: Remote work during the COVID-19 pandemic may have relaxed company expectations concerning employees' business attire. But it raised expectations for employees' qualifications, work experience, skills and educational credentials when hiring, according to new research from Northeastern Unive

items.title: Light-activated compound kills antibiotic-resistant bacteria by turning its own defense enzyme against it
items.summary: Antibiotic resistance is becoming an accelerating crisis because of the overuse and misuse of antibiotics over many years. The problem is exacerbated when antibiotics wipe out susceptible bacteria but leave resistant bacteria behind to multiply, further spreading resistance. There is an ongoing sear

items.title: Decline in plankton across Northeast Atlantic sends stark warning for ocean health
items.summary: Microscopic plankton are among the most important organisms on Earth. Phytoplankton produce around half of the oxygen we breathe, while plankton as a whole underpin marine food webs, support fisheries, help regulate carbon and sustain life across the ocean.

items.title: Inviting students to shape support systems can improve mental health and campus environments
items.summary: By listening to students and partnering with them to shape solutions, Dr. Adam Alvarez's research shows that schools can build trust, reduce isolation and create supportive communities where students feel valued.

items.title: Giraffes combine quantities similarly to addition
items.summary: In addition to humans, some species of primates and birds have demonstrated under experimental conditions their ability to manipulate quantities in tasks that require combining or separating them, in a manner similar to addition or subtraction. Now, a study by the University of Barcelona, published

items.title: Tiny DNA 'hitchhikers' may be reshaping life in thawing Arctic soils
items.summary: Amid the peatlands of northern Sweden, billions of microbes are quietly rewriting their genetic playbooks—and doing so far more often than scientists realized.

items.title: Deadly Venezuela earthquakes raise concern in tremor-prone California
items.summary: In the aftermath of back-to-back earthquakes in northern Venezuela, which by Friday had killed more than 500 people and left thousands injured, experts in resilience planning have emphasized the increasing importance of disaster preparedness in earthquake-prone California.

items.title: Europa's ice shell secrets unlocked by ground radar study
items.summary: Jupiter's moon Europa has become high-value real estate for astrobiologists and the search for life beyond Earth. This is because the small moon, which is slightly smaller than Earth's moon, boasts a massive subsurface ocean of liquid water that scientists estimate contains about double th

items.title: NASA Astronaut Chris Williams Preps for Spacewalk
items.summary: Flight engineer Sophie Adenot of ESA (European Space Agency) helps flight engineer Chris Williams of NASA as he tries on his spacesuit on June 23, 2026, testing its comfort and mobility as well as its communications and life support systems inside the International Space Station’s Quest airlock. Wil

items.title: Mapping Earth’s Observations, featuring Betsy Ford
items.summary: NASA’s Earth-observing satellites track an enormous range of phenomena: how aerosols move through the atmosphere, how moisture descends through soil, how land-cover shifts over decades. It’s some of the most consequential data NASA produces, informing science, policy, agriculture, and cl

items.title: Functional NIN persists in non-nodulating plants: Rethinking the loss of symbiosis
items.summary: Certain plants, including legumes, form specialized root organs known as nodules. These plants establish symbiotic relationships with nitrogen-fixing bacteria and utilize atmospheric nitrogen. This process, termed "root nodule symbiosis," is an important biological function that can reduce reliance

items.title: Childbirth is not uniquely difficult to humans
items.summary: The tight fit of a baby's head through a mother's birth canal, which causes great difficulty in childbirth, is not unique to humans, as previously understood. Instead, some small-bodied primate babies have heads almost twice as large as their mothers' pelvic space, a new study led by

items.title: NextSTEP-3 A: Lunar Enabling Technology
items.summary: Solicitation Number: 80GRC026R0008 May 19, 2026 – Synopsis issued June 29, 2026 – Draft BAA and Appendix A Issued | News Release NASA issued a draft Broad Agency Announcement under NextSTEP‑3, Appendix A, on June 29, 2026, to advance concepts that accelerate the technological readiness of criti

items.title: NASA Seeks Industry Input to Accelerate Lunar Surface Technologies
items.summary: Long-term lunar exploration requires technology, infrastructure, and operations that function together cohesively on the surface of the Moon. To accelerate the development of key lunar surface systems and reduce risk, NASA and industry must work together in the design, development, testing, and eval

items.title: Exposure to violence contributes to high rates of suicidal thoughts and attempts among transgender people
items.summary: A new analysis from the Williams Institute at UCLA School of Law of the 2022 U.S. Transgender Survey data finds that among respondents ages 16 and older, 39% reported serious suicidal thoughts in the year before the survey and 5% had attempted suicide. In comparison, the 2022 National Survey of Drug

items.title: Ultra-faint galaxy discovered near Andromeda may be 12.5 billion years old
items.summary: A new ultra-faint dwarf galaxy has been discovered in the vicinity of Andromeda (M31), the Milky Way's large neighboring galaxy. The new study, published in Astronomy & Astrophysics suggests that the galaxy, named And XXXVI, is one of the faintest satellite galaxies discovered around Andromeda

items.title: UV light patterns thermochromic crystals without damage, unlocking color-changing designs
items.summary: Color-changing mood rings, forehead fever strips and car-shade indicators all change hues as they warm and cool, thanks to a phenomenon called thermochromism. On a smaller scale, thermochromism is used in nanotechnologies like sensors, electronics and computing. These applications require smart mate

items.title: Online calculator shows how drastically mowing affects insects
items.summary: How many insects and spiders live in 1 square meter (11 square feet) of meadow? What impact do humans have on this biodiversity in mowed meadows, lawns and roadside verges? A new online tool answers these questions: the Insect Calculator, developed by researchers at TU Darmstadt.

items.title: Age bias discovered in AI
items.summary: Do responses generated by artificial intelligence systems such as ChatGPT reflect social prejudice? A KAIST research team has quantitatively analyzed and identified age-related stereotypes embedded in the responses of generative artificial intelligence. The study sheds light on the potential impact

items.title: Red-tailed hawks maintain flight performance despite missing feathers
items.summary: Red-tailed hawks can compensate for feather loss during molt by subtly changing their wing and tail movements, according to a new study by University of California, Davis, researchers in the College of Engineering and the Weill School of Veterinary Medicine.

items.title: DNA databases unite to create a fully open resource for transposable element research
items.summary: For more than three decades, researchers studying genomes have relied on foundational resources such as Repbase and, more recently, Dfam to identify and classify transposable elements—the mobile DNA sequences that shape genome structure, evolution and function. Now, Dfam and Repbase are coming toget

items.title: Nanopore technology identifies proteins molecule by molecule
items.summary: Proteins are responsible for most functions in the human body. However, their analysis, which is essential for understanding diseases, developing drugs and discovering new biomarkers, remains highly complex. Using a technology called "nanopore detection," a team at the University of Geneva (UNIGE) h

items.title: Why nanoscale droplets don't coalesce and microscale droplets do
items.summary: Olive oil and water do not naturally mix. Water molecules are polar, having a net electric dipole moment due to the bend angle of about 104.5° between the two oxygen-hydrogen bonds. Olive oil is nonpolar due to its long hydrocarbon chains, which makes it hydrophobic and insoluble in water. Mixtures

items.title: This tiny organism contracts 200 times faster than we can blink—here's how
items.summary: A tiny, aquatic, single-celled organism can contract to one-quarter of its body length in less than 5 milliseconds—hundreds of times faster than a human can blink. Researchers have discovered that the organism, Spirostomum ambiguum, uses a calcium-activated protein network in a fishnet-like configur

items.title: Camera traps reveal the true culprit behind crop damage in Honduras
items.summary: A new study from the Honduran Mosquitia shows how simple, noninvasive technology can help solve one of the most common challenges in wildlife conservation: identifying the species actually responsible for crop damage. The full detailed results have been published in Neotropical Biology and Conservat

items.title: Giant exoplanet may hold a magnetic grip on its host star
items.summary: Within their planetary systems, stars are continuously shaping their orbiting planets through gravity, radiation and magnetic forces. So far, this relationship has appeared to be a one-way street.

items.title: Closing the AI fluency gap to support workforce retention
items.summary: University of Phoenix College of Doctoral Studies has published a new white paper, "The Retention Mandate: Bridging the AI Fluency Gap to Secure the 2026 Workforce," authored by Wayne L. McCoy, DM, MBA, and released through the Center for Educational and Instructional Technology Research (CEITR).

items.title: Mummified dogs reveal Tiwanaku people buried companions beside homes long before they became status symbols
items.summary: In the arid landscapes of southern Peru, around 1,100 years ago, someone carefully dug a small pit, laid down a woven mat and placed a young dog within as if sleeping, possibly wrapped in twine. Centuries later, the mummified remains would be one of only two intentionally buried mummified dogs from

items.title: Toward experiment-guided AlphaFold: Researchers overcome AI tool's single-conformation limitation
items.summary: The AI-based program AlphaFold predicts a protein's 3D structure with remarkable accuracy. However, it tends to reduce heterogeneous structures to a single dominant conformation, or shape, and overlooks experimental conditions that can alter local structure. Researchers at the Institute of Scie

items.title: Astronomi otkrili odakle dolazi tajanstvena "sablasna čestica"
items.summary: ASTRONOMI su ušli u trag visokoenergetskom neutrinu do 11 milijardi svj. godina udaljene galaksije 'Shadow Blaster'. Otkriće je omogućila gravitacijska leća koja je pojačala svjetlost galaksije.

items.title: Nakon 40 godina identificiran prvi fosil dinosaura s Antarktike
items.summary: FOSIL pronađen na Antarktici 1985. i zaboravljen 40 godina, identificiran je kao repna kost titanosaura. To je prva kost dinosaura ikad pronađena na tom kontinentu.

items.title: Ponovno pronađen fosil megalodona potvrđuje njegovu divovsku veličinu
items.summary: PONOVNO pronađen najveći poznati kralježak megalodona, izgubljen desetljećima. Fosil iz Danske potvrđuje da je prapovijesni morski pas mogao doseći duljinu do 24 metra i loviti druge morske pse.

items.title: NASA želi spriječiti pad teleskopa od 1,4 tone na Zemlju. Šalje robota u svemir
items.summary: SWIFT od lansiranja 2004. godine promatra svemir i bilježi neke od najsnažnijih eksplozija u svemiru.

items.title: The order of species loss alters how grasslands maintain stability, study finds
items.summary: Grasslands account for roughly 40% of terrestrial ecosystems and are paramount to global food security. Wild grasslands provide food for livestock and habitat for pollinators and act as a carbon sink in the era of climate change. Maintaining the health of native grasslands is a critical component of

items.title: Star-Spangled City
items.summary: Two hundred years ago, a battle in the port city of Baltimore inspired the writing of America’s national anthem.

items.title: Brain activity under anesthesia challenges what we know about consciousness
items.summary: The unconscious brain appears to be far more capable than scientists once believed. Researchers found that patients under general anesthesia could still process language at a sophisticated level, distinguishing nouns, verbs, and adjectives while listening to stories. Even more remarkably, neural act

items.title: These tiny soil microbes could rescue crops from salty farmland
items.summary: Researchers have discovered that beneficial soil bacteria give plants an unexpected survival advantage in salty soils. Instead of helping plants keep salt out, the microbes stimulate the production of lignin, a natural compound that strengthens roots and makes plants more resilient. Greenhouse and f

items.title: The oldest evidence of mourning rituals reveals Paleolithic communities grieved like we do
items.summary: Roughly 27,500 years ago, a 15-year-old boy was brutally mauled by a bear in Arene Candide in what is now Liguria, Italy. The attack tore through his jaw, neck and left shoulder. He was dying, but he was not alone in his final moments.

items.title: Lost megalodon vertebrae resurface, confirming 80-foot size estimate
items.summary: An associated set of gigantic vertebrae belonging to the iconic extinct megalodon, or megatooth shark, that had been missing in action since the 1980s was discovered, providing new information about the shark's lifestyle. Two Museum of Southern Jutland staff members, Mette Elstrup and Trine Sør

items.title: Human activity has driven retreat of Antarctica's fastest melting glacier
items.summary: Human-driven climate change significantly intensified the retreat of one of the most important glaciers in Antarctica during the 20th century. The Pine Island Glacier, which drains a large part of the West Antarctic Ice Sheet into the Amundsen Sea, is one of the biggest contributors to global sea le

items.title: Bird flu is deadly for backyard chickens—and even cats. A vet expert explains
items.summary: The deadly H5N1 strain of bird flu has been found in seabirds in two Australian states.

items.title: The solar gravitational lens could map white dwarfs and black holes
items.summary: It feels like every few months we get to report on another academic paper singing the praises of the Solar Gravitational Lens (SGL). Partly, this is due to Dr. Slava Turyshev's astounding productivity in pumping out academic articles, but partly because such a groundbreaking mission has lots of

items.title: Europe swelters as heat wave moves east
items.summary: Europe's deadly heat wave pushed east Sunday, with hundreds of millions still sweltering across the continent despite fleeting relief from overnight storms, notably in France and Belgium.

items.title: Nanopattern method unlocks precise control of disorder for wave-guiding devices
items.summary: A research team has developed a methodology to precisely design and control the "degree of disorder" in nanopattern arrays using metal-infiltrated block copolymer (BCP) thin films. The work was led by Professor So Youn Kim of the Seoul National University College of Engineering Department of Chemica
